title: SEGMENT 10 — MSI-high and the first tumor-agnostic approval

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — MSI-high and the first tumor-agnostic approval
Welcome back.
Let me pick up where we left off, because the story of MSI-high cancers and the first tumor-agnostic approval deserves its own segment.
The tumor-agnostic approval — pembrolizumab for any MSI-high or dMMR solid tumor — happened in May 2017.
To understand why this was such a big deal, you have to understand how oncology had organized itself for the previous hundred years.
Cancer drugs had always been approved organ-by-organ.
Tamoxifen was a breast cancer drug.
Imatinib was a chronic myeloid leukemia drug, then later a GIST drug — but those were separate approvals, separate trials, separate indications.
Each new tumor type required its own pivotal trial.
If you had a rare cancer, even one that was molecularly similar to a common cancer, you often had no approved therapy because nobody had done a trial in your tumor.
The MSI-high approval shattered that paradigm.
The FDA said, in effect: we are approving this drug not for a tumor type but for a molecular feature that crosses tumor types.
If your cancer is MSI-high, regardless of whether it's in your colon, your uterus, your pancreas, your stomach, or your prostate, you qualify for pembrolizumab.
Why MSI?
The answer goes back to the biology.
A tumor with broken mismatch repair accumulates mutations at an extraordinary rate.
The genome becomes a snowstorm of small insertions, deletions, and substitutions.
Many of those mutations alter protein sequences.
Each altered protein gets chopped up and displayed on MHC.
The tumor becomes covered in neoantigens — strange peptides the T cells have never seen before, and were never trained to tolerate.
In an immunocompetent host, those neoantigens are enormous red flags.
T cells recognize them, infiltrate the tumor, attempt to destroy it.
And the tumor, in classic fashion, defends itself by upregulating PD-L1 and other suppressive signals.
The T cells are present, but suppressed.
This is the perfect setup for a PD-1 inhibitor.
There is an enormous, already-primed T-cell army parked inside the tumor.
All you have to do is take off the brakes, and the army does the rest.
The clinical results, when this was tested, were extraordinary.
In Dung Le's first major report, in 2015, about half of MSI-high colorectal cancer patients had objective responses to pembrolizumab.
In the broader basket trial that supported the 2017 approval, response rates across thirty-plus different tumor types were in the thirty-to-fifty percent range.
Some patients had complete responses lasting years.
In a population where the alternative was chemotherapy with a median survival of a year or two, this was transformative.
A few practical points worth knowing.
First, MSI-high is over-represented in certain hereditary cancer syndromes.
Lynch syndrome — the most common hereditary colon cancer predisposition — is caused by germline mutations in the mismatch repair genes.
About three percent of all colorectal cancers and two percent of all endometrial cancers are Lynch syndrome cancers, all of them MSI-high.
People with Lynch syndrome are now routinely tested, and their cancers are immunotherapy-responsive in a way that was unimaginable a decade ago.
Second, the MSI-high signal extends to early-stage disease.

In 2022, a small but landmark study by Andrea Cercek and colleagues at Memorial Sloan Kettering treated patients with locally advanced MSI-high rectal cancer with dostarlimab — another anti-PD-1 antibody — for six months.
All twelve initial patients had complete responses.
Every single one.
No surgery, no chemoradiation, just six months of an immune checkpoint inhibitor, and the tumors disappeared.
The trial has been expanded and the results continue to hold.
We are looking at the possibility that, for MSI-high rectal cancer, surgery and radiation may no longer be necessary at all.
The immunotherapy is curative on its own.
Third, the principle has been extended.

Following the MSI-high precedent, the FDA has approved several other tumor-agnostic indications — TMB-high tumors for pembrolizumab, NTRK fusions for larotrectinib and entrectinib, BRAF V600E for dabrafenib-trametinib, RET fusions for selpercatinib.
Each of these approvals says: the genetic signature defines the treatment, not the tissue of origin.
We are entering an era in which a patient's cancer is increasingly defined by its molecular fingerprint, and treatment follows the fingerprint wherever it leads.
The MSI-high story is the cleanest example of this shift, and it is one of the proudest moments of the modern era of cancer therapy.
A biology insight — broken mismatch repair generates neoantigens, neoantigens prime T cells, T cells are released by PD-1 blockade — has produced cures in cancers that were previously incurable.
And it has dragged the entire FDA regulatory framework into a new way of thinking.
